9.2.2.8.1 Anchorage of mechanical devices
Mechanical or other devices conforming to IS 16712 capable of developing the strength of the bar without damage to concrete may be used as anchorage with the approval of the engineering-charge. At least the minimum straight length shall be provided before mechanical device as specified in IS 16712.
9.2.2.8.2 Anchorage in shear reinforcement
Notwithstanding any provisions of this standard, in transverse reinforcement (such as stirrups and transverse ties), complete development lengths and anchorage shall be deemed to have been provided when the bar is bent around a bar of at least its own diameter ¢g, through an angle of at least 90°, 135° and 180° and is continued beyond the end of the curve for a length of at least 8¢,, 6¢, and 4¢,, respectively, as specifiedin IS 2502.
9.2.2.8.3 Bearing stresses at bends
The bearing stress f, in concrete inside bends and hooks complying with IS 2502 need not be checked. Otherwise, the bearing stress inside a bend shall be estimated as:

9.2.2.8.4 Other requirements
Further,
a) If a change in direction of tension or compression reinforcement induces a resultant force acting outward tending to split the concrete, such force should be taken up by additional links or stirrups. Additional requirements are specified in Liquid retaining structures in IS 3370.
b) Bent tension bar at a re-entrant angle should be avoided.
9.2.2.9 Splicing of bars
Where splices are provided in the reinforcing bars, they shall be away (as far as possible) from the sections of maximum stress and be staggered. Splices in flexural members shall not be at sections, where the bending moment demand Mp is more than 50 percent of the limiting moment capacity M_.
When bars are required to be spliced:
a) Lap Splicing
1) Shall not be used in concrete members carrying axial tension,
2) May be used in concrete members in flexural tension,
3) May be used in concrete members in axial or flexural compression, and
4) Shall be avoided to the extent possible of longitudinal bars of diameters of 16mm and higher in flexural members. Mechanical couplers complying with the requirements of IS 16712 shall be used instead;

9.2.2.6 Minimum spacing of bars
9.2.2.6.1 Reinforced concrete members
In all concrete members (such as slabs, beams, columns, walls, shafts and shells), the minimum spacing of bars shall be as specified hereunder:
a) The horizontal distance between two parallel main bars in beams, columns and walls shall be not less than the largest of the following:
1) Diameter of a bar, if the diameters are equal,
2) Diameter of the bar of the largest size, if the diameters are unequal, and
3) 5mm more than the maximum nominal size of coarse aggregate.
The vertical distance between any two layers (see Fig. 25) shall be not less than the largest of the following:
1) Diameter of a bar, if the diameters are equal,
2) Diameter of the bar of the largest size, if the diameters are unequal,
3) 5mm more than the maximum nominal size of coarse aggregate, and
4) 16mm.
¢) Where needle vibrators are required to pass through the horizontal bars to vibrate the concrete in the core of the member, then the said distance between the parallel main bars or groups of bars shall be sufficient to enable to pass the vibrator (at the angle needed).

9.2.2.6.2 Prestressed concrete members
a) Grouping of cables or ducts in post-tensioned bonded cables
1) Bundles shall not be permitted of more than two ducts each.
2) If there is possibility of obstruction of flow of concrete or in severe exposure condition, horizontal grouping should be avoided.
3) A pair of ducts placed horizontally or vertically touching each other shall be permitted, only in the straight portion of the cable subject to limitations given below (Fig. 26)
i) Two cables can be grouped horizontally provided diameter of each duct is not more than 50 mm.
ii) Two cables can be grouped vertically provided diameter of each duct is not more than 110 mm.
iii) Two cables shall not be bundled over the curved length of cable in the plane of curvature.
b) The horizontal and vertical distances between ducts of groups of cables, ducts of cables, or tendons shall not be less than the greater of the following:
1) Post-Tensioning - Bonded or Unbonded Embedded Cables (Fig. 26):
i) Outer diameter of a duct, between single ducts or between a pair of ducts and the next pair;
ii) 50mm, and
ili) Maximum nominal size of coarse aggregate plus 10 mm.
The above shall be applicable for major lengths of the cables. These requirements can be relaxed locally near transition lengths of the cable, provided the minimum clear spacing between the adjacent cables along the line joining the centers of two cables is not less than the smaller of outer diameter of the duct and 50 mm.

9.4.3.6.2 In general, where the torsional stiffness (and strength) of members has not been considered in the analysis of the structure, no specific calculations for torsion shall be necessary. Adequate control in torsional cracking shall be assumed to have been provided by the required nominal shear reinforcement. But, where the torsional stiffness (and strength) of members has been considered in the analysis, the members shall be designed for torsion.
9.4.3.6.3 These provisions shall be applicable only when:
a) Solid rectangular beams with D>b,
b) Hollow rectangular beams with D> b and wall thickness t>b/4,
c) Solid T-beams and I-beams, whose webs comply with the requirement of D>b.
a) Limiting Stress
A concrete member shall resist the serviceability design torsional moment demand Tsp at the critical section by concrete, transverse reinforcement steel and longitudinal steel. T,,, shall be considered to increase both the serviceability design shear force demand V,, and the serviceability design bending moment demand M,, . These additional demands on V,, and M,, shall be taken as specified hereunder.
Under the action of shear force, at the critical section of a RC member:
1) Contribution of Concrete:
The nominal shear stress demand 17,,, in concrete shall not exceed the permissible shear stress 7,; and
shear stress 7,; and
(ii) Contribution of Transverse Reinforcing Steel:
The tension in transverse reinforcing steel stirrups shall not exceed the Permissible Tensile Stress o,,.
Also, in the estimation of the Serviceability Design Shear Force Capacity V, of a section, it shall be assumed that both concrete and transverse steel reach simultaneously these said permissible stresses.

A blast imposes two hazards, namely blast loading and missile impact. Protecting the asset from both of these hazards disintegrating and collapsing the structure shall be the central theme of design. But, the provisions of this standard address only the effects of the former and not of the latter. It is the intent of this standard to mitigate the possible damage in a structure, which depends on its proximity to the blast. The following possible effects shall be addressed depending on the proximity of the structure to the blast:
a) Close proximity:
1) Collapse of a part of the building,
2) Progressive collapse of a part of the building, or
3) Progressive collapse of the whole building;
b) Medium proximity: Failure (that is collapse or deterioration) of:
1) Structural members of the exterior bay, like beams, columns, walls and slabs; and
2) Non-structural elements, along the facade of the building closest to the

9.3.3.3 Columns and Beam-Columns
The general requirements for geometry shall be provided as specified in 9.2.
a) Design
Columns shall be designed for axial force, shear force and bending moment to resist all forces transferred thorough them under the combination of loads specified in Table 3 for Strength Criteria. The capacity of the columns shall be estimated as specified in 9.3.2.4 and 9.3.2.7 at least at the critical sections specified in 9.2.5.
b) Detailing
The general requirements for reinforcement shall be provided as specified in 9.2. In addition, the requirements given hereunder shall be complied with.
A RC compression member shall have
(1) Longitudinal steel in columns enclosed within closed loop transverse reinforcement for effective lateral restraint, in compliance with the general requirements specified in 9.2.
(2) Transverse or helical reinforcement so disposed that every longitudinal bar nearest to the compression face has effective lateral support against buckling subject to the following provisions:
(a) The effective lateral support is given by transverse reinforcement either in the form of circular rings capable of taking up circumferential tension or by polygonal links (lateral ties) with internal angles not exceeding 135°. The ends of the transverse reinforcement shall be properly anchored.
(b) If the longitudinal bars are not spaced more than 75 mm on either side, transverse reinforcement need only to go round comer and alternate bars for the purpose of providing effective lateral supports (Figure 63(a)).
(c) If the longitudinal bars spaced at a distance of not exceeding 48 times the diameter of the tie are effectively tied in two directions, additional longitudinal bars in between these bars shall be tied in one direction by open ties (Figure 63(b)).
276
context_text.same_page:
FOR BIS USE ONLY
CED 02(27096)P January 2025
(d) Where the longitudinal bars in a compression member are grouped (not in contact) and each group adequately tied with transverse reinforcement as specified in 9.2.2, the transverse reinforcement for the compression member as a whole may be provided on the assumption that each group is a single longitudinal bar for purpose of determining the pitch and diameter of the transverse reinforcement as specified in 9.2.2. The diameter of such transverse reinforcement need not exceed 20 mm.
(3) Helical reinforcement shall be used only in piles, and not in columns, and when used, they shall comply with the following:
(a) Pitch - Helical reinforcement shall be of regular formation with the turns of the helix spaced evenly and its ends shall be anchored properly by providing one and a half extra turns of the spiral bar. Where an increased load on the column on the strength of the helical reinforcement is allowed for, the pitch of helical turns shall be not more than 75 mm, nor more than 1/6 of the core diameter of the column, nor less than 25 mm, nor less than 3 times the diameter of the steel bar forming the helix. In other cases, the requirements of 9.2.2 shall be complied with.
(b) Diameter - The diameter of the helical reinforcement shall be in accordance with 9.2.2.1.

(4) In columns where longitudinal bars are offsets at a splice,
(a) the slope of the inclined portion of the bar with the axis of the column shall not exceed 1 in 6, and the portions of the bar above and below the offset shall be parallel to the axis of the column.
(b) Adequate horizontal support at the offset bends shall be treated as a matter of design, and shall be provided by metal ties, spirals, or parts of the floor construction. Metal ties or spirals so designed shall be placed near (not more than 8 times bar diameter from) the point of bend. The horizontal thrust to be resisted shall be assumed as 1.5 times the horizontal components of the nominal stress in the inclined portion of the bar.
(c) Offset bars shall be bent before they are placed in the forms. Where column faces are offset 75 mm or more, splices of vertical bars adjacent to the offset face shall be made by separate dowels overlapped as specified in 9.2.2.8 (Figure 64).

(5) Openings in Flat Slabs
Openings of any size may be provided in the Flat Slab, if it is shown by detailed analysis that the requirements of strength and serviceability are met. But, when openings conform to the following, no special analysis is required.
a) Openings of any size may be placed within the middle strip in each direction, provided the total amount of reinforcement required for the middle strip without the opening is provided in the remaining width.

8.2.3.1 Linear Analysis
8.2.3.1.1 Linear analysis may be performed of concrete structural systems, when:
a) Stability index Q,<0.04, wherein Q, is estimated as per 8.2.3 under the combinations of loads given in 6.4 for strength criteria (Table 3); and
b) Slenderness ratios kL/r of all columns satisfy:
KL _ tio Columns NOT braced against side - sway r |40 Columns braced against side - sway
where
k = Effective length factor of the column,
L = Length of the column, and
r = Radius of gyration
in which I, and Ag are the gross second moment of area and gross area of cross- section of the column section, respectively. r for a column of rectangular cross- section may be taken as 0.30 times the dimension of cross-section in the considered direction of deformation, and of circular cross-section as 0.25 times the diameter of circular cross-section.
8.2.3.1.2 Linear analysis may be performed using the four methods, namely:
a) Frame Method shall be used when structures consist largely of members that have one dimension much larger than their other two.
b) Finite Element Method shall be used when structures consist of members that are stocky and structures have only structural walls and slabs. The appropriateness of the results from this analysis shall be ensured before considering them in design. Stress concentration at points of application of concentrated loads, re-entrant angles, and openings in slabs, shall be addressed in the design stage.
85
context_text.next_page:
FOR BIS USE ONLY
CED 02(27096)P January 2025
c) Hybrid Method shall be used when structures consist of mixed structural systems and members of uneven geometry (such as, deep beams and/or beam-column joints with similar sizes). Some members may be modelled as frame members by their centerline geometries (such as, beams and columns) and others by 3- dimensional finite elements (such as, beam-column joints, structural walls, and service cores in buildings). All requirements related to modelling of the structure specified in the Frame and Finite Element Methods shall be applicable here.
d) Strut & Tie Method shall be used when structures consist of complex load paths, non-uniform stress distribution in non-prismatic members and junctions of members of concrete structures. This method is meant to perform local analysis, and shall not be used as a substitute for overall global structural analysis of the structure.
8.2.3.1.3 In each of methods mentioned above, it shall be ensured that:
a) Geometry (such as areas of cross-sections and moments of inertia) of members is consistent with 8.2.1.1(a), and
b) Modulus of elasticity is considered as specified in 7.1.4.1, 7.2.2.1 and 7.3.1.2.1.
c) Mass is represented accurately, when dynamic actions are to be assessed.
d) Boundary conditions reflect the true restraint at the supports.
e) Effects of shear deformations are considered when estimating the stiffness of members whose L/D ratio is less than 10. This requirement shall not be applicable to slabs.
8.2.3.2 Approximate Geometric Nonlinear Analysis
Geometric nonlinear analysis shall be performed of laterally flexible concrete structural systems (like frame structures), if any, when 0.04<Q, <0.10, wherein Q; is estimated as per 8.2.3 under the combinations of loads given in 6.4 for strength criteria (Table 3). The approximate geometric nonlinear analysis may be performed to estimate the additional bending moments (due to P—A effects) on columns as per 8.3.1.7, and used in the design of columns.
8.2.3.3 Nonlinear Analysis
Material and geometric nonlinear analysis shall be performed of laterally flexible concrete structural systems, if any, when Q; >0.10, wherein Q, is estimated as per 8.2.3. Nonlinearities accruing in the structure shall be considered under the action of the loads specified in the load combinations mentioned in 6.4, when checking for the compliance of the structure with robustness, integrity and restorability criteria.

7.3.2.4 Sheathing duct joints
7.3.2.4.1 The sheathing ducts shall be of the spiral corrugated type. For major projects, the sheathing ducts shall be manufactured preferably at the project site utilizing appropriate machines. With such an arrangement, long lengths of sheathing ducts may be used with consequent reduction in the number of joints and couplers. Where sheathing duct joints are unavoidable, such joints shall be made cement slurry tight by the use of corrugated threaded sleeve couplers which can be tightly screwed on to the outer side of the sheathing ducts. A heat-shrink coupler also could be used, if suitable.
7.3.2.4.2 The sleeve coupler (see Fig. 6) shall not have a length of the coupler less than 150mm, but should be increased up to 200 mm wherever practicable. The joints between the ends of the coupler and the duct shall be sealed with adhesive sealing tape to prevent penetration of cement slurry during concreting. The couplers of adjacent ducts shall be staggered, wherever practicable. As far as possible, couplers shall not be located in curved zones. The corrugated sleeve couplers are being manufactured conveniently using the sheath making machine with the next higher size of die set.
15088 ——|
FIG. 6 TYPICAL DETAILS OF SLEEVE COUPLER
7.3.2.4.3 The heat-shrink coupler (see Fig. 7) is supplied in the form of bandage rolls, which can be used for all diameters of sheathing ducts. The bandage is coated on the underside with a heat sensitive adhesive so that after heating the bandage material shrinks in to the sheathing and ensures formation of a leak proof joint, without the need for extra taping or support in the form of corrugated sleeve couplers. A soft gas flame is used to heat the coupler.

7.3.3 Mechanical Couplers
7.3.3.1 Fixed or movable type mechanical couplers shall:
a) Be placed to not adversely affect the load carrying capacity of the member;
b) Meet the specifications laid down by the manufacturer for minimum concrete cover over couplers and reinforcement for bursting and spalling, but subjected to acceptance tests similar to those specified in clause. for anchorages;
c) Be located away from intermediate supports;
d) Not be used in more than on 50 percent of the tendons at any cross-section;
e) Be spaced such that the distance between any two successive sections at which cables are coupled is not closer than 1.5m in structural members whose depth is less than 2.0m, but not closer than 3.0m when depth is more than 2.0m;
f) Meet the requirements of strength of individual anchorages as specified in 9.2.2.7; and
g) Be able to transfer full force of tendon from one to another.
7.3.3.2 Also, the anchorage and stressing of second tendon shall not disturb the anchorage of the first tendon when fixed couplers are used.
7.4 Prestressing Systems
Prestressing systems manufactured by specialist manufacturers shall be compatible with the standard prestressing wires/strands used. The stressing equipment, de- stressing/restressing facilities, and grouting arrangements shall be compatible with the tendons.

9.3.5 Design of Concrete Structures with Prestressed Members
When concrete structures are prestressed, the following shall apply:
a) In buildings with prestressed slabs and beams, the lateral drift of the building shall meet the requirement given in 7.1.4 of IS 1893 (Part 2).
b) Buildings with prestressing shall not be used in slabs and beams, the lateral drift of the building shall meet the requirement given in 7.1.4 of IS 1893 (Part 2).
9.3.6 Design and detail by strut and tie method
Local parts and junctions of members of concrete structures can be analysed and designed by the strut and tie method. When doing so, the provisions hereunder shall be complied with.
Structural concrete members or local regions in such members shall be:
a) Modelled as an idealized truss consisting of struts and ties as per 8.2.1,
b) Analysed as a truss subjected to the factored loads as per 8.2.3,
c) Designed to resist the forces so obtained as per 9.3.6.1, and
d) Detailed with reinforcement bars to meet the intent of design as per 9.3.6.2.

c) Tension Load Test
A test sample (1,100 mm long) shall be hung from a vertical support, so that the sample is supported at all points of outward corrugations (see Fig. 4). The hollow core is filled with a wooden circular piece having a diameter of 95 percent of the inner diameter of the sample to maintain circular profile during test loading (see Fig. 4). The sample is said to be acceptable, if there is no deformation of the joints or slippage of couplers.

8.7.2 Ribbed, Hollow Or Voided Slabs
The bending moments and shear forces due to design loads on continuous slabs shall be obtained by the rigorous method specified in 9.3.3.1(e).
8.7.3 Flat Slabs
8.7.3.1 General
The coefficients given hereunder are for slabs subjected to gravity loads only. Values provided hereunder cannot be added numerically with those obtained by other methods of analysis. The effects of lateral loads on flat slabs shall be estimated by detailed nonlinear analysis (using hybrid analysis specified in 8.1.3.1).
The term flat slab means a RC slab without beams or with beams not meeting the requirements of I/d ratio specified in 8.8.1, but with or without drops, which is supported by columns with or without flared column heads or capitals (Fig. 15). It may be solid slab or may have recesses formed on the soffit, so that the soffit comprises a series of ribs in two directions. The recesses may be formed by removable or permanent filler blocks.
8.7.3.1.1 For the purpose of this clause, the following definitions shall apply:
a) Column strip - a strip of slab having a width of 0.25, but not greater than 0.25], on each side of the column centerline, where 1, is the span in the direction moments are being determined, measured centre to centre of supports and I, the span transverse to 1, measured centre to centre of supports.
b) Middle strip - a strip of slab bounded on each of its opposite sides by the Column Strip.
c) Panel strip - a part of a slab bounded on each of its four sides by the centerline of a column, or centerlines of adjacent spans.
101

8.7.3.2 Geometric proportioning
8.7.3.2.1 Thickness of flat slab
The minimum thickness of slab shall be 125 mm. The thickness of a flat slab shall be controlled by considerations of span to effective depth ratios given by 8.2.3.1.
For slabs with drops conforming to 8.7.3.2.2, the ratios of longer span to effective depth given in 8.2.3.1 shall be applied directly, else the ratios shall be multiplied by 0.9. For this purpose, only the longer span shall be considered.
8.7.3.2.2 Drop
When drops are provided, they shall be rectangular in plan, and have a length in each direction not less than one-third of the panel length in that direction. For exterior panels, the width of drops at right angles to the non-continuous edge and measured from the centerline of the columns shall be equal to one-half the width of drop for interior panels.
8.7.3.2.3 Column heads
When column heads or capitals are provided, the portion of a column head that lies within the largest right circular cone or pyramid that has a vertex angle of 90° and can be included entirely within the outlines of the column and the column head, shall be considered for design purposes (see Fig. 15).
102
context_text.next_page:
FOR BIS USE ONLY
CED 02(27096)P January 2025
8.7.3.3 Determination of bending moment
8.7.3.3.1 Methods of analysis
Flat slab systems shall be analysed by the computer-based finite element method. In the absence of results from such detailed analysis, it shall be permissible to analyse such systems by one of the following methods:
a) The direct design method as specified in (8.7.3.4) and
b) The equivalent frame method as specified in (8.7.3.5)
subject to the limitations of the methods mentioned therein.
8.7.3.3.2 Bending moments in panels with marginal beams
When the slab is supported by a marginal beam of depth more than 1.5 times the thickness of the slab or by a wall, then:
a) The total load to be carried by the beam or wall shall comprise those loads applied directly on the wall or beam plus a uniformly distributed load equal to one-quarter of the total load on the slab, and
b) The bending moments on the half-column strip adjacent to the beam or wall shall be one-quarter of the bending moments for the first interior column strip.
8.7.3.3.3 Transfer of bending moments to columns

For resisting the bending moment arising in the slab, a slab width between lines that are 1.5D on each side of the column or capital shall be considered effective, where D is the larger of: (i) thickness of slab, (ii) thickness of drop panel, or (iii) width of column resisting the bending moment. Concentration of reinforcement over column head by closer spacing or additional reinforcement may be used to resist the bending moment at this section.

9.3.5 Design of Concrete Structures with Prestressed Members
When concrete structures are prestressed, the following shall apply:
a) In buildings with prestressed slabs and beams, the lateral drift of the building shall meet the requirement given in 7.1.4 of IS 1893 (Part 2).
b) Buildings with prestressing shall not be used in slabs and beams, the lateral drift of the building shall meet the requirement given in 7.1.4 of IS 1893 (Part 2).
9.3.6 Design and detail by strut and tie method
Local parts and junctions of members of concrete structures can be analysed and designed by the strut and tie method. When doing so, the provisions hereunder shall be complied with.
Structural concrete members or local regions in such members shall be:
a) Modelled as an idealized truss consisting of struts and ties as per 8.2.1,
b) Analysed as a truss subjected to the factored loads as per 8.2.3,
c) Designed to resist the forces so obtained as per 9.3.6.1, and
d) Detailed with reinforcement bars to meet the intent of design as per 9.3.6.2.

9.3.2.7.8 Structural Walls
9.3.2.7.8.1 The design axial load Pp and design bending moments Mpy and Mpy on RC structural walls shall be estimated as in biaxial bending of columns specified in 9.3.2.7. In particular:
a) In the out-of-plane direction, structural walls shall be treated as slender columns and the same provisions applicable for columns (specified in 9.3.2.7 shall be complied with, including the effect of additional moments; and
b) In the in-plane direction, additional moments shall not be used for estimating the total moment in the in-plane direction.

9.2.6 Design Strengths of Concrete and Steels
This standard refers to three types of strengths, namely characteristic strength, design strength, and overstrength. The design strength fp of concrete, reinforcing steel and prestressing steel shall be obtained as:
fo = Sf characterétic , YM
where
S characteristic
= characteristic strength of the material specified in 7.1, 7.2 and
7.3, and
YM = partial safety factor appropriate for material and the loading action being considered.
The design strengths of concrete and steels shall be used in the design of the structural concrete sections and members.
Higher grades of materials than that used in design shall not be used in beams.
9.2.6.1 Concrete
In the design of concrete sections and members, design lengths of lap splices and design development lengths (including design lengths of anchorage), the following seven design strengths of concrete shall be considered as specified hereunder:
a) Limiting design bearing strength f,,,,
b) Limiting design bond strength 7,,,,
c) Limiting design axial strength f,,, in axial compression, and limiting design axial strength f,,; in axial tension,
d) Limiting design shear stress capacity z,, and
e) Limiting design flexural strength f,,,. in bending compression and limiting design flexural strength f,,; in bending tension.
Designers may use values lower than those specified hereunder.

a) Flexure-dominant Beams
(1) DESIGN
Beams shall be designed for Shear Force V and Bending Moment M to resist all forces transferred thorough them under the combination of loads specified in Table 3 for Strength Criteria. The capacity of the Beams shall be estimated as specified in 9.3.2.4 and 9.3.2.5 at least at the critical sections specified in 9.2.5.
(2) DETAILING
The general requirements for reinforcement shall be provided as specified in 9.2. In addition, the requirements given hereunder shall be complied with. Longitudinal steel in beams shall be enclosed within closed loop transverse reinforcement for effective lateral restraint. The arrangement of transverse reinforcement shall be as below:
(i) Longitudinal Compression Reinforcement
Whenever compression reinforcement is introduced in a beam to resist additional moments (and not merely as hanger bars to hold the longitudinal reinforcement bars), it should be restrained effectively within closed loop transverse reinforcement. Also, in flanges of T-and L-beams, where flanges are in tension, a part of the main tension reinforcement shall be distributed over the effective flange width or a width equal to 1/10" of the span, whichever is smaller. If the effective flange width exceeds 1/10 of the span, nominal longitudinal reinforcement shall be provided in the portions of the flange beyond 1/10" of the span.

(ii) Side Face Reinforcement
Where the depth of the clear length of the web in a beam exceeds 750 mm, side face reinforcement shall be provided along both two faces of the clear length of the web. The total area of such reinforcement shall be not less than 0.05% of the web area on each face. This steel shall be distributed equally a spacing not exceeding 300 mm or web thickness, whichever is less.
(iii) Transverse reinforcement in beams for Shear and Torsion
The transverse reinforcement in beams shall be taken around the outer-most tension and compression longitudinal bars. And,
(a) The transverse reinforcement for torsion shall be rectangular closed stirrups placed perpendicular to the axis of the member. The spacing of the stirrups shall not exceed Min x)" 300m | , where x; and y, are the short and long dimensions of the stirrup, respectively.

9.3.3.5.2 Raft Foundations
Raft foundations shall comply with the following:
a) Longitudinal flexural reinforcement in raft foundations shall be placed in two curtains, one on top face and another on the bottom face, along both plan directions. This shall meet the minimum longitudinal reinforcement requirements specified in 9.2.4.1(a) (6).
b) In one-way rafts, flexural shear shall be designed for across the full width of the raft.
c) In two-way rafts, both flexural shear and punching shear shall be designed for mandatorily.
9.3.3.5.3 Piles and Pile Caps
The requirements specified in IS 2911 (Part 1/Sec 1) shall be complied with.
9.3.3.5.4 Piled-Rafts
The requirements specified in IS 2911 (Parts 1 and 3) shall be complied with.
9.3.3.5.5 Wells and Well Caps
The relevant specifications of Indian Roads Congress shall be complied with.
9.3.3.6 Special Structural Elements
Adequate end anchorage shall be provided for tension reinforcement in flexural members where reinforcement stress is not directly proportional to moment, such as sloped, stepped, or tapered footings, brackets, deep beams and members in which the tension reinforcement is not parallel to the compression face.
9.3.3.6.1 Staircases
The recommendations related to strength, deflection and crack control for beams and slabs shall be applicable for staircases also. Further, the following shall be applicable.
a) Distribution of loads on stairs
In stairs with open wells, where spans partly crossing at right angles occur, the load on areas common to any two such spans may be taken as 50 percent in each direction as shown in Fig. 69(a). Where flights or landings are embedded into walls for a length of not less than 110 mm and are designed to span in the direction of
290
context_text.same_page:
FOR BIS USE ONLY
CED 02(27096)P January 2025
the flight, a 150 mm strip may be deducted from the loaded area and the effective breadth of the section increased by 75 mm for purposes of design [see Fig. 69(b)].
b) Depth of section
The depth of section shall be taken as the minimum thickness perpendicular to the soffit of the staircase.
c) Modeling of staircases
Staircases shall be modeled to capture the stiffness introduced between two successive floor slabs by the inclined slab and/or inclined spine beams. But, when sliding joint detailing is adopted at the junction of the floor slab and inclined slab and/or inclined spine beams, the analytical model shall be modified accordingly to not introduce any stiffness between two successive floor slabs.

9.3.3.6.2 Corbels
9.3.3.6.2.1 Corbels may be designed by using strut &tie method. When doing so, the following shall be complied with:
a) The inclination of strut with respect to the main tie shall lie between 45° and 68°.
b) The strut shall be designed such that the concrete compression stress in it does not exceed that given in 9.3.6.
c) The reinforcement, corresponding to the ties shall be fully anchored beyond the node under the bearing plate, by using U-hoops or anchorage devices such as couplers, unless a length I, ,,.; is available between the face of the vertical member and the front of the corbel. |, ,,.; shall be measured beyond the full width of
291
context_text.next_page:
FOR BIS USE ONLY
CED 02(27096)P January 2025
compressive strut, and shall be fully anchored at the other end of the vertical member to which the corbel is attached.
9.3.3.6.2.2 Alternately, a corbel may be designed by the following prescriptive method, if the following conditions are satisfied:
a) The distance a, of application of factored design vertical force V, is less than its effective depth d determined at the face of the vertical member (Fig. 70),
b) The Corbel is subjected to a net downward load V;, and
c) The Corbel is subjected to factored design horizontal force Hp acting away from the vertical member is less than V-.
9.3.3.6.2.3 In the prescriptive method, a corbel shall be designed in accordance with the following provisions:
a) The design of the Corbel shall depend on the geometry of the corbel. If:
1) a<0.5D: The Corbel shall be provided with closed horizontal stirrups with area A,, shall be provided in addition to the main tension reinforcement Aj, at the top of the Corbel. Here, A,,, shall comply with:
Agy > 0.5A;,
which shall be provided in upper two-third of the corbel depth d.
2) 0.5D<a<D: The Corbel shall be provided with closed vertical stirrups with area Agcy shall be provided in addition to the main tension reinforcement at the top of the Corbel and the closed horizontal or inclined stirrups. Here, Agy shall comply with:
_ 0.5Vp 0.87 f, ; SV
Also, the Corbel shall be designed such that the concrete compression stress in the strut does not exceed that given in 9.3.6.
3) a>D: The Corbel shall be designed as a cantilever flexural member.
b) Ifthe corbel has varying depth, then the depth dy at the outside edge of the corbel shall not be less than 0.5d, half the depth at the face of the support. In such a case, The favourable contributions from inclined compression chord shall not be considered when resisting the factored loads Vp and Hr.
c) Critical section at the face of the support shall be designed to resist simultaneously Vr, H,,and a factored moment Vya, + H-(h-d).

8.7.2 Ribbed, Hollow Or Voided Slabs
The bending moments and shear forces due to design loads on continuous slabs shall be obtained by the rigorous method specified in 9.3.3.1(e).
8.7.3 Flat Slabs
8.7.3.1 General
The coefficients given hereunder are for slabs subjected to gravity loads only. Values provided hereunder cannot be added numerically with those obtained by other methods of analysis. The effects of lateral loads on flat slabs shall be estimated by detailed nonlinear analysis (using hybrid analysis specified in 8.1.3.1).
The term flat slab means a RC slab without beams or with beams not meeting the requirements of I/d ratio specified in 8.8.1, but with or without drops, which is supported by columns with or without flared column heads or capitals (Fig. 15). It may be solid slab or may have recesses formed on the soffit, so that the soffit comprises a series of ribs in two directions. The recesses may be formed by removable or permanent filler blocks.
8.7.3.1.1 For the purpose of this clause, the following definitions shall apply:
a) Column strip - a strip of slab having a width of 0.25, but not greater than 0.25], on each side of the column centerline, where 1, is the span in the direction moments are being determined, measured centre to centre of supports and I, the span transverse to 1, measured centre to centre of supports.
b) Middle strip - a strip of slab bounded on each of its opposite sides by the Column Strip.
c) Panel strip - a part of a slab bounded on each of its four sides by the centerline of a column, or centerlines of adjacent spans.

a) Structural Design
The grades of materials (namely concrete and reinforcing steel) admissible for use in concrete structures shall be as specified in 1.2. Further, in the calculations related to design of transverse reinforcement, the characteristic yield strength f, shall not be taken to be more than 415 MPa, irrespective of the grade of transverse steel used.
The admissible combinations of the grades of concrete and reinforcing steel in reinforced concrete members shall comply with those specified by Table 51. The said combinations shall be applicable for both longitudinal and transverse steels.
b) Reinforcement Detailing
The detail requirements provided in this standard are mandatory for basic structural components and sub-systems. Members of concrete structures shall be provided with reinforcing steel at locations and faces where concrete member is likely to undergo axial tension, bending tension, axial compression, and/or bending compression. This reinforcement shall be arranged along longitudinal and transverse directions of the member.
Bars may be arranged singly, or in pairs in contact, or in groups of three or four bars bundled in contact. Bundled bars shall be enclosed within stirrups or ties. Bundled bars shall be tied together to ensure the bars remaining together. Bars larger than 32 mm diameter shall not be bundled, except in columns.
The recommendations for detailing for earthquake-resistant construction given in IS 13920 should be taken into consideration, where applicable.

9.2.2.6.2 Prestressed concrete members
a) Grouping of cables or ducts in post-tensioned bonded cables
1) Bundles shall not be permitted of more than two ducts each.
2) If there is possibility of obstruction of flow of concrete or in severe exposure condition, horizontal grouping should be avoided.
3) A pair of ducts placed horizontally or vertically touching each other shall be permitted, only in the straight portion of the cable subject to limitations given below (Fig. 26)
i) Two cables can be grouped horizontally provided diameter of each duct is not more than 50 mm.
ii) Two cables can be grouped vertically provided diameter of each duct is not more than 110 mm.
iii) Two cables shall not be bundled over the curved length of cable in the plane of curvature.
b) The horizontal and vertical distances between ducts of groups of cables, ducts of cables, or tendons shall not be less than the greater of the following:
1) Post-Tensioning - Bonded or Unbonded Embedded Cables (Fig. 26):
i) Outer diameter of a duct, between single ducts or between a pair of ducts and the next pair;
ii) 50mm, and
ili) Maximum nominal size of coarse aggregate plus 10 mm.
The above shall be applicable for major lengths of the cables. These requirements can be relaxed locally near transition lengths of the cable, provided the minimum clear spacing between the adjacent cables along the line joining the centers of two cables is not less than the smaller of outer diameter of the duct and 50 mm.

i) Three times the diameter of a wire/tendon;
ii) 20 mm, and
iii) mm more than the maximum nominal size of coarse aggregate.
c) Cables or ducts may be grouped together vertically in groups of not more than two.

9.2.5.2.2 Reinforced concrete beams
When beams carry uniformly distributed load, or the principal load is a concentrated load and located at a distance more than 2d from the face of the support (even though the reaction due to the applied load introduces compression in the support), the critical section for shear force shall be at:
a) The face of the support, when the member is supported from below [Fig. 36(a)] (such that the reaction due to applied loads introduces compression in the support); and
b) A distance d from the face of the support, when the member is supported from above [Fig. 36(b)] (such that the reaction due to applied loads introduces tension in the support). Here, sections located at a distance less than d from the face of the support shall be designed for the same shear force as that computed at a distance d from the face of the support,

7.3.2.4 Sheathing duct joints
7.3.2.4.1 The sheathing ducts shall be of the spiral corrugated type. For major projects, the sheathing ducts shall be manufactured preferably at the project site utilizing appropriate machines. With such an arrangement, long lengths of sheathing ducts may be used with consequent reduction in the number of joints and couplers. Where sheathing duct joints are unavoidable, such joints shall be made cement slurry tight by the use of corrugated threaded sleeve couplers which can be tightly screwed on to the outer side of the sheathing ducts. A heat-shrink coupler also could be used, if suitable.
7.3.2.4.2 The sleeve coupler (see Fig. 6) shall not have a length of the coupler less than 150mm, but should be increased up to 200 mm wherever practicable. The joints between the ends of the coupler and the duct shall be sealed with adhesive sealing tape to prevent penetration of cement slurry during concreting. The couplers of adjacent ducts shall be staggered, wherever practicable. As far as possible, couplers shall not be located in curved zones. The corrugated sleeve couplers are being manufactured conveniently using the sheath making machine with the next higher size of die set.
15088 ——|
FIG. 6 TYPICAL DETAILS OF SLEEVE COUPLER
7.3.2.4.3 The heat-shrink coupler (see Fig. 7) is supplied in the form of bandage rolls, which can be used for all diameters of sheathing ducts. The bandage is coated on the underside with a heat sensitive adhesive so that after heating the bandage material shrinks in to the sheathing and ensures formation of a leak proof joint, without the need for extra taping or support in the form of corrugated sleeve couplers. A soft gas flame is used to heat the coupler.

7.3.2.4.3.1 Corrugated HDPE sheathing ducts
A summary of all the requirements, methods of verification, and acceptance criteria for the polymer duct system together with the components and material is given in Table 21.
7.3.3 Mechanical Couplers
7.3.3.1 Fixed or movable type mechanical couplers shall:
a) Be placed to not adversely affect the load carrying capacity of the member;
b) Meet the specifications laid down by the manufacturer for minimum concrete cover over couplers and reinforcement for bursting and spalling, but subjected to acceptance tests similar to those specified in clause. for anchorages;
c) Be located away from intermediate supports;
d) Not be used in more than on 50 percent of the tendons at any cross-section;
e) Be spaced such that the distance between any two successive sections at which cables are coupled is not closer than 1.5m in structural members whose depth is less than 2.0m, but not closer than 3.0m when depth is more than 2.0m;
f) Meet the requirements of strength of individual anchorages as specified in 9.2.2.7; and
g) Be able to transfer full force of tendon from one to another.
7.3.3.2 Also, the anchorage and stressing of second tendon shall not disturb the anchorage of the first tendon when fixed couplers are used.
7.4 Prestressing Systems
Prestressing systems manufactured by specialist manufacturers shall be compatible with the standard prestressing wires/strands used. The stressing equipment, de- stressing/restressing facilities, and grouting arrangements shall be compatible with the tendons.

8.7.3.3 Determination of bending moment
8.7.3.3.1 Methods of analysis
Flat slab systems shall be analysed by the computer-based finite element method. In the absence of results from such detailed analysis, it shall be permissible to analyse such systems by one of the following methods:
a) The direct design method as specified in (8.7.3.4) and
b) The equivalent frame method as specified in (8.7.3.5)
subject to the limitations of the methods mentioned therein.
8.7.3.3.2 Bending moments in panels with marginal beams
When the slab is supported by a marginal beam of depth more than 1.5 times the thickness of the slab or by a wall, then:
a) The total load to be carried by the beam or wall shall comprise those loads applied directly on the wall or beam plus a uniformly distributed load equal to one-quarter of the total load on the slab, and
b) The bending moments on the half-column strip adjacent to the beam or wall shall be one-quarter of the bending moments for the first interior column strip.

9.2.2.6.2 Prestressed concrete members
a) Grouping of cables or ducts in post-tensioned bonded cables
1) Bundles shall not be permitted of more than two ducts each.
2) If there is possibility of obstruction of flow of concrete or in severe exposure condition, horizontal grouping should be avoided.
3) A pair of ducts placed horizontally or vertically touching each other shall be permitted, only in the straight portion of the cable subject to limitations given below (Fig. 26)
i) Two cables can be grouped horizontally provided diameter of each duct is not more than 50 mm.
ii) Two cables can be grouped vertically provided diameter of each duct is not more than 110 mm.
iii) Two cables shall not be bundled over the curved length of cable in the plane of curvature.
b) The horizontal and vertical distances between ducts of groups of cables, ducts of cables, or tendons shall not be less than the greater of the following:
1) Post-Tensioning - Bonded or Unbonded Embedded Cables (Fig. 26):
i) Outer diameter of a duct, between single ducts or between a pair of ducts and the next pair;
ii) 50mm, and
ili) Maximum nominal size of coarse aggregate plus 10 mm.
The above shall be applicable for major lengths of the cables. These requirements can be relaxed locally near transition lengths of the cable, provided the minimum clear spacing between the adjacent cables along the line joining the centers of two cables is not less than the smaller of outer diameter of the duct and 50 mm.

7 STRUCTURAL MATERIALS
The minimum requirements given hereunder shall be achieved on site by ingredients, grades, testing, physical properties, mix, composition, reinforcing steel and prestressing steel so as to meet requirements of design and detailing specified in this standard.
Structural materials shall comply with the requirements laid down in this standard and in other relevant national standards, so that the required performance of the structure is assured as envisaged throughout the design service life of structures built using these structural materials.
7.1 Concrete
The ingredients, grades, methods of testing, physical properties and mix proportions of concrete shall comply with the requirements given hereunder.
7.1.1 Ingredients of Concrete
23
context_text.next_page:
FOR BIS USE ONLY
CED 02(27096)P January 2025
The basic ingredients of concrete shall be chosen based on the specifications specified hereunder.
7.1.1.1 Aggregates
a) Normal weight aggregates — Aggregates derived from natural sources and other than natural sources (manufactured aggregates such as iron slag, copper slag, bottom ash from thermal power plants, recycled concrete aggregates and recycled aggregates), shall comply with the requirements of IS 383. These are natural or synthetic aggregates with in-place density (unit weight) between 22.40 to 27.00 kN/mé.
NOTE — For aggregates having unit weight more than 24.00 kKN/m3, higher unit weight than mentioned in IS 875 (Part 1) need to be considered depending upon the actual unit weight of aggregates.
b a Heavyweight aggregates — Natural or synthetic aggregates with in-place density (unit weight) more than 27.00 kN/m®and can range up to 45.00 kN/m® for aggregates other than steel. Steel aggregates (if used) will have further higher density. Heavy weight aggregate is most commonly used for radiation shielding, counterweights and other applications where a high mass-to-volume ratio is desired. Goethite, Limonite, Barite, Illmenite, Magnetite, Hematite, Ferrophosphorus and Steel are some examples of heavy weight aggregate.
Specialist literature may be referred for designing concrete structures made using heavy weight aggregates (see also 12).
The engineering properties and durability of concrete made with heavy weight aggregates are different than normal concrete. Therefore the properties and design formulae given in this code (for normal concrete) shall not be applicable. Specialist literature should be consulted.
c) Lightweight aggregates — Natural or synthetic with in-place density (unit weight) between 14.40 to 18.40 kN/m°. The lightweight is due to the cellular or high internal porous structure, which gives this type of aggregate a low specific gravity. The most important aspect of lightweight aggregate is the porosity. They have high absorption values, which requires a modified approach to concrete proportioning. Light weight aggregates namely sintered fly ash aggregates may be used for making light weight structural concrete (a type of special concrete). Sintered flyash coarse aggregates when used shall conform to IS 9142 (Part 2).
Specialist literature may be referred for designing concrete structures made using heavy weight aggregates (see also 12).
The engineering properties and durability of concrete made with light weight aggregates are different than normal concrete. Therefore the properties and

And, the bending moment resisted by the concrete member when the member reaches the said limit state at a section shall be taken as the design bending moment capacity M, of that section.
9.3.2.4.2 Slender beams
RC slender beams, slabs, footings and rafts shall be designed to be under-reinforced with steel reaching the limiting strain in tension before concrete reaches the limiting strain in compression. The design bending moment capacity M; of RC beams shall be obtained by solving the following set of governing nonlinear equations:
a) Equilibrium of forces and moment at a section,
b) Compatibility of strains at a section, and
c) Constitutive Law of constituent materials.
Because the constitutive law of concrete is nonlinear, the solutions shall be obtained iteratively.
9.3.2.4.2.1 The estimation of limiting moment capacity M,, of a doubly-reinforced rectangular RC section designed to be under-reinforced shall be as given hereunder (Fig. 42).

9.3.2.4.3 Slabs, footings and rafts
The design bending moment capacity M);, of RC slabs, footings and rafts shall be estimated by the same method as that in RC beams, but considering unit width of the slab as width b of the beam for the purpose of calculations.

a) Even PSC beams shall be designed to behave in a ductile manner with prestressing steel reaching the limiting strain in tension before concrete reaching the limiting strain in compression. The effect of prestressing steel alone shall be considered in the estimation of limiting moment capacity M), of prestressed beams; the contribution to Mr, shall be neglected of the untensioned longitudinal reinforcing steel.

9.2.3.2 Cross-sectional aspect ratio
a) Beams
The maximum D/b ratio of the cross-section of a beam without a compression flange shall be 2.5.
b) Columns
The maximum D/b ratio of a column shall be 2.5. Columns with D/b ratio is in the range 2.5 to 4.0 are discouraged. When used under compulsion, they shall be designed and detailed as columns, and not as structural walls.
c) Structural Walls
The minimum L,,/t,, ratio of a structural wall shall be 4<(L,,/t,,)<20.
9.2.3.3 Distinction between columns and structural walls
Notwithstanding, the provisions of 8.2.3.1 and 8.2.3.2, a vertical member shall be designed as a structural wall, if it has the bending moment diagram about its major axis along its full height like that of a vertical cantilever in single curvature along its full height [Fig. 31(a)], or at best one point of contra-flexure owing to frame-wall interaction (especially at the roof level), even though with small jumps at the levels where the beams and slabs frame into it [Fig. 31(c)]. The bending moment at the roof level need not be zero, owing to the presence of beams at that level.
Else, the vertical member shall be designed as column, if it is in double curvature along the whole or part of its height [Fig. 31(b)].

9.2.4 Amount of Reinforcements
The minimum and maximum amounts of reinforcements in concrete structures shall be taken as 9.2.4.1 and 9.2.4.2, respectively. Longitudinal reinforcement bars shall be provided to carry the axial force and bending moments imposed on the members. The transverse reinforcement bars shall be provided to carry shear forces imposed on the members, and to hold the longitudinal bars without buckling under its design levels

Bars may be arranged singly, or in pairs in contact, or in groups of three or four bars tied together in contact. The cross-sections where bars are bundled shall be enclosed within closed stirrups. Also, bundled bars shall be tied together to ensure the bars remaining together during concreting. Bars larger than 32 mm diameter shall not be bundled, except in columns.
The recommendations for earthquake-resistant design and detailing of concrete structures in earthquake zones III, IV, V and VI shall be as per IS 13920 (Parts 1 and 2: Sec 3).

b) Transverse Load Rating Test
A test sample (500 mm long) shall be placed on a horizontal support, so that the sample is supported at all points of outward corrugations (see Fig. 3), and loaded gradually in increments (as per Table 21) at the centre of the supported portion through a circular contact surface of 12 mm diameter. The sample is said to be acceptable, if the permanent deformation is less than 5 percent of the original diameter.

(d) Where the longitudinal bars in a compression member are grouped (not in contact) and each group adequately tied with transverse reinforcement as specified in 9.2.2, the transverse reinforcement for the compression member as a whole may be provided on the assumption that each group is a single longitudinal bar for purpose of determining the pitch and diameter of the transverse reinforcement as specified in 9.2.2. The diameter of such transverse reinforcement need not exceed 20 mm.
(3) Helical reinforcement shall be used only in piles, and not in columns, and when used, they shall comply with the following:
(a) Pitch - Helical reinforcement shall be of regular formation with the turns of the helix spaced evenly and its ends shall be anchored properly by providing one and a half extra turns of the spiral bar. Where an increased load on the column on the strength of the helical reinforcement is allowed for, the pitch of helical turns shall be not more than 75 mm, nor more than 1/6 of the core diameter of the column, nor less than 25 mm, nor less than 3 times the diameter of the steel bar forming the helix. In other cases, the requirements of 9.2.2 shall be complied with.
(b) Diameter - The diameter of the helical reinforcement shall be in accordance with 9.2.2.1.

(4) In columns where longitudinal bars are offsets at a splice,
(a) the slope of the inclined portion of the bar with the axis of the column shall not exceed 1 in 6, and the portions of the bar above and below the offset shall be parallel to the axis of the column.
(b) Adequate horizontal support at the offset bends shall be treated as a matter of design, and shall be provided by metal ties, spirals, or parts of the floor construction. Metal ties or spirals so designed shall be placed near (not more than 8 times bar diameter from) the point of bend. The horizontal thrust to be resisted shall be assumed as 1.5 times the horizontal components of the nominal stress in the inclined portion of the bar.
(c) Offset bars shall be bent before they are placed in the forms. Where column faces are offset 75 mm or more, splices of vertical bars adjacent to the offset face shall be made by separate dowels overlapped as specified in 9.2.2.8 (Figure 64).

9.3.3.3 Columns and Beam-Columns
The general requirements for geometry shall be provided as specified in 9.2.
a) Design
Columns shall be designed for axial force, shear force and bending moment to resist all forces transferred thorough them under the combination of loads specified in Table 3 for Strength Criteria. The capacity of the columns shall be estimated as specified in 9.3.2.4 and 9.3.2.7 at least at the critical sections specified in 9.2.5.
b) Detailing
The general requirements for reinforcement shall be provided as specified in 9.2. In addition, the requirements given hereunder shall be complied with.
A RC compression member shall have
(1) Longitudinal steel in columns enclosed within closed loop transverse reinforcement for effective lateral restraint, in compliance with the general requirements specified in 9.2.
(2) Transverse or helical reinforcement so disposed that every longitudinal bar nearest to the compression face has effective lateral support against buckling subject to the following provisions:
(a) The effective lateral support is given by transverse reinforcement either in the form of circular rings capable of taking up circumferential tension or by polygonal links (lateral ties) with internal angles not exceeding 135°. The ends of the transverse reinforcement shall be properly anchored.
(b) If the longitudinal bars are not spaced more than 75 mm on either side, transverse reinforcement need only to go round comer and alternate bars for the purpose of providing effective lateral supports (Figure 63(a)).
(c) If the longitudinal bars spaced at a distance of not exceeding 48 times the diameter of the tie are effectively tied in two directions, additional longitudinal bars in between these bars shall be tied in one direction by open ties (Figure 63(b)).
276

5.2.1 Strength Criteria
5.2.1.1 Performance requirements
The structure (or a part thereof) shall possess sufficient:
a) Strength to sustain only limited or no damage under the action of loads applied as per basic load combinations specified in Table 3,
b) Strength to resist inelastic effects, under the action of loads applied as per accidental load combinations specified in Table 3, and
c) Deformation capacity that results in no collapse of the structure, either partially or completely, under the action of loads applied as per extreme load combinations specified in Table 3.
5.2.1.2 Design requirements
The structure shall comply with the following:
a) Strains in concrete and steel at a point in the critical cross-sections of a member shall be within the limiting values specified in 9.3.2.3 and 9.3.2.4 when estimating the limiting design axial force and limiting design bending moment capacities respectively, or a combination thereof; and
b) Stresses in concrete and steel at a point in the critical cross-sections of a member shall be within the limiting values specified in 9.3.2.5 and 9.3.2.6 when estimating the limiting design shear force and limiting design torsional moment capacities, respectively.
5.2.1.3 The detailed requirements for strength criteria are given at 9.3.
5.2.2 Serviceability Criteria
5.2.2.1 Performance requirements
The structure (or a part thereof) shall possess sufficient stiffness to ensure that:
a) Stresses induced at the critical sections do not result in significant nonlinearities in concrete and steel, or initiate cracking at a material point level under fatigue
b) Crack wits do not expose reinforcement bars resulting in excessive corrosion, an
c) Deformations do not damage the architectural elements and utilities,
under the action of loads applied as per basic load combinations given in Table 4.

9.3.3.3 Columns and Beam-Columns
The general requirements for geometry shall be provided as specified in 9.2.
a) Design
Columns shall be designed for axial force, shear force and bending moment to resist all forces transferred thorough them under the combination of loads specified in Table 3 for Strength Criteria. The capacity of the columns shall be estimated as specified in 9.3.2.4 and 9.3.2.7 at least at the critical sections specified in 9.2.5.
b) Detailing
The general requirements for reinforcement shall be provided as specified in 9.2. In addition, the requirements given hereunder shall be complied with.
A RC compression member shall have
(1) Longitudinal steel in columns enclosed within closed loop transverse reinforcement for effective lateral restraint, in compliance with the general requirements specified in 9.2.
(2) Transverse or helical reinforcement so disposed that every longitudinal bar nearest to the compression face has effective lateral support against buckling subject to the following provisions:
(a) The effective lateral support is given by transverse reinforcement either in the form of circular rings capable of taking up circumferential tension or by polygonal links (lateral ties) with internal angles not exceeding 135°. The ends of the transverse reinforcement shall be properly anchored.
(b) If the longitudinal bars are not spaced more than 75 mm on either side, transverse reinforcement need only to go round comer and alternate bars for the purpose of providing effective lateral supports (Figure 63(a)).
(c) If the longitudinal bars spaced at a distance of not exceeding 48 times the diameter of the tie are effectively tied in two directions, additional longitudinal bars in between these bars shall be tied in one direction by open ties (Figure 63(b)).

9.3.2.4.6 Columns and structural walls
RC columns and structural walls shall be designed for combined effects of design axial force Py and design bending moment Mp considering P,—M,, interaction of the member (as specified in 9.3.2.7), and not just for the effect of design bending moment Mp . When structural walls are provided with boundary elements, the said calculations shall be performed considering the full cross-section as a single entity; the cross-section shall not be considered separately as web and boundary elements and principle of superposition applied.
9.3.2.5 Design shear force capacity V;,
A concrete member shall resist the design shear force demand Vp at the critical section by both concrete and transverse reinforcement steel.
9.3.2.5.1 Limiting stress
Under the action of shear force, at the critical section of a reinforced concrete or prestressed concrete member:
a) Contribution of concrete
Concrete shall be assumed to have reached its limiting stress of r, , the design shear strength of concrete; and
b) Contribution of transverse reinforcing steel
Transverse reinforcing steel shall be assumed to have reached its limiting stress of 0.87 f,, the design tensile strength of reinforcing steel.
And, the shear force resisted by the concrete member when the member reaches these said limiting stresses simultaneously at a section shall be taken as the design shear force capacity V;, of that section. In flanged sections, only the width of the web shall be

9.2.3.2 Cross-sectional aspect ratio
a) Beams
The maximum D/b ratio of the cross-section of a beam without a compression flange shall be 2.5.
b) Columns
The maximum D/b ratio of a column shall be 2.5. Columns with D/b ratio is in the range 2.5 to 4.0 are discouraged. When used under compulsion, they shall be designed and detailed as columns, and not as structural walls.
c) Structural Walls
The minimum L,,/t,, ratio of a structural wall shall be 4<(L,,/t,,)<20.
9.2.3.3 Distinction between columns and structural walls
Notwithstanding, the provisions of 8.2.3.1 and 8.2.3.2, a vertical member shall be designed as a structural wall, if it has the bending moment diagram about its major axis along its full height like that of a vertical cantilever in single curvature along its full height [Fig. 31(a)], or at best one point of contra-flexure owing to frame-wall interaction (especially at the roof level), even though with small jumps at the levels where the beams and slabs frame into it [Fig. 31(c)]. The bending moment at the roof level need not be zero, owing to the presence of beams at that level.
Else, the vertical member shall be designed as column, if it is in double curvature along the whole or part of its height [Fig. 31(b)].

9.2.4 Amount of Reinforcements
The minimum and maximum amounts of reinforcements in concrete structures shall be taken as 9.2.4.1 and 9.2.4.2, respectively. Longitudinal reinforcement bars shall be provided to carry the axial force and bending moments imposed on the members. The transverse reinforcement bars shall be provided to carry shear forces imposed on the members, and to hold the longitudinal bars without buckling under its design levels

Also, bundled bars shall be tied together to ensure the bars remaining together during concreting. Bars larger than 32 mm diameter shall not be bundled, except in columns.

9.2.1.2 Effective depth
The effective depth d of a reinforced or prestressed concrete member (other than a deep beam) shall be taken as the distance between the centroid of the area of tension reinforcement and the outermost fiber on the compression face, excluding the thickness of any material not cast monolithically with the member and of any concrete provided to allow for wear. This shall be applicable to slabs, shallow beams, columns and structural walls, but not to deep beams and corbels. For deep beams, lever arm shall be used instead of effective depth, as per 9.3.2.2(c).

9.3 DESIGN FOR STRENGTH
The design for strength of a structure shall be performed by designing each of its members along with the joints and connections between them to resist the elongation/compression, bending, shearing or twisting (and the consequent stress- resultants) imposed on them by the probable most unfavourable combination of loads applied on the structure as specified in Table 3 (with appropriate partial safety factors 7,, for loads in each combination).
The design strength of a member at a section (including the effect of partial safety factors ym for material strengths) shall be more than the corresponding demand associated with each stress-resultant or in combination, and shall be estimated as its resistance corresponding to when any of its constituent materials reaches a limit state (either of strain or stress) specified hereunder.
199

FOR BIS USE ONLY
CED 02(27096)P January 2025
9.3.1 Stability
The structure shall meet the following requirements related to stability.
a) Equilibrium stability
A structure shall meet the provisions of overall stability specified in 9.1 before its members, joints and connections are designed for strength.
Elastic stability
Each member (namely beam, column and structural wall) shall meet the maximum slenderness ratios specified in 9.2.3.1 before it is designed for strength. Failure resulting from elastic instability in beams and columns can be avoided by complying with 9.2.3.1 and 9.2.3.2, and accounting for effective lengths of members under axial, bending and combined axial-bending actions by suitably proportioning its size (as per 9.2.3), and accounting for its bending moment capacity for the axial load appearing on it (as specified in 9.3.2.3 and 9.3.2.4).
Plastic stability
The structure shall meet the provisions specified in 5.7 and 5.8 under the actions of loads combined as per Table 6 and 6.4.
9.3.2 Design strength capacities
The design strength capacities of sections of concrete members shall be those corresponding to the limit states of:
a) strain specified in 9.3.2.3 and 9.3.2.4 when estimating axial force and bending moment capacities, respectively; and
b) stress specified in 9.3.2.5 and 9.3.2.6 when estimating shear force and torsion moment capacities, respectively.
All concrete members provided with steel reinforcement bars, or steel reinforcement bars and prestressing steel, and subjected to:
a) axial force and bending moment shall be designed to behave in an ductile manner; and

h) Disconnection shall be facilitated by a short length of flexible tube connects the duct and injection pipe. This may be squeezed and cut off after the grout has hardened.
10.6.3.3.5 Precautions and recommendations for effective grouting
The following shall be ensured:
a) In cold and frosty weather, injection of grout shall be postponed unless special precautions are taken. If frost is likely to occur within 48 h after injection, heat shall be applied to the member and maintained for at least 48 h after injection, so that the temperature of the grout does not fall below 5°C. Prior to commencement of grout, care shall be taken to ensure that the duct is completely free of frost/ice by flushing with warm water but not with steam.
When the ambient temperature during the day is likely to exceed 35°C, grouting shall be done in the early morning or late evening hours.
) When the cables are threaded after concreting, the duct shall be temporarily protected during concreting by inserting a stiff rod or a rigid PVC pipe or any other suitable method.
During concreting, care shall be taken to ensure that the sheathing is not damaged. Needle vibrators shall be used with extreme care by well experienced staff only, to ensure the above requirements.
Prestressing cables shall be moved in both directions during the concreting operations. This can be done by light hammering the ends of the wires/ strands during concreting. Also, 3 to 4 h after concreting, the cable should be moved both ways through a distance of about 200 mm. With such movement, any leakage of mortar, which has taken place in spite of all precautions loses bond with the cables, thus reducing the chance of blockages. This operation can be done also by fixing prestressing jacks at one end pulling the entire cable and then repeating the operation by fixing the jack at the other end.
The cables to be grouted shall be separated by as much distance as possible.
8) In case of stage prestressing, cables tensioned in the first stage shall not remain ungrouted till all cables are stressed. It is a good practice, while grouting any duct in stage prestressing, to keep all the remaining ducts filled up with water containing 1 percent lime or by running water through such ducts till the grout has set. After grouting the particular cable, the water in the other cables shall be drained and removed with compressed air to prevent corrosion.
zz Care shall be taken to avoid leaks from one duct to another at joints of precast members.
End faces where anchorages are located are vulnerable points of entry of water. They have to be necessarily protected with an effective barrier. Recesses shall be packed with mortar concrete and preferably be painted with water proof paint.
k) After grouting is completed, the projecting portion of the vents shall be cut off and the face protected to prevent corrosion.

10.7 Untensioned Steel Bars In Prestressed Concrete Structures
All requirements of steel reinforcement bars in RC structures are applicable for untensioned steel in PSC structures.
10.8 Special Considerations
10.8.1 Construction Joints
Construction joints (also called cold joints) shall be avoided to the extent possible. If this is not possible, their number shall be minimized. Concreting shall be carried out continuously up to construction joints, the position and arrangement of which shall be indicated by the designer. Construction joints should comply with IS 11817.
10.8.1.1 Construction joints shall not be located on the portion subjected to large shear force. The direction of construction joints is crossed at right angles to the direction of compressive force in member. When construction joints are located on the portion subjected to shear force:
a) Ifshear resistance to be mobilized is moderate at the construction joint, notches shall be provided and the construction joint shall be reinforced appropriately; and
b) If shear resistance to be mobilized is large at the construction joint, shear keys may be provided.
10.8.1.2 Construction joints shall be placed at accessible locations to permit cleaning out of laitance, cement slurry and unsound concrete, to create rough or uneven surface. Laitance and cement slurry shall be cleaned out using wire brush on the surface of joint immediately after initial setting of concrete and to clean out the same immediately thereafter. The prepared surface shall be in a clean saturated surface dry condition, when fresh concrete is placed against it.
10.8.1.3 In the case of construction joints at locations where the previous pour has been cast against shuttering, a rough surface for the previously poured concrete shall be obtained to expose the aggregate with a high pressure water jet or any other appropriate means. Fresh concrete shall be thoroughly vibrated near construction joints so that mortar from the new concrete flows between large aggregates and develops proper bond with old concrete.
10.8.1.4 When leaving dowels open in structural members at a construction joint for some time before continuing the construction, precautions shall be taken. Cement slurry shall be applied on the bars during the exposed period. When steel bars are showing the signs of rusting, they shall be cleaned properly before continuing the construction. Anti-corrosive treatment may be done, if necessary.

9.3.2.6 Design torsional moment capacity T,,
9.3.2.6.1 General
a) A member in torsion is said to have reached its limit state, when concrete at a section reaches the corresponding design shear strength r, of concrete and reinforcing steel stirrup at that section the corresponding characteristic tensile strength fp.
b) All the eight assumptions listed in 9.3.2.1 shall be applicable when estimating the design torsional moment capacity also.
c) In general, where the torsional stiffness (and strength) of members has not been considered in the analysis of the structure, no specific calculations for torsion shall be necessary. Adequate control in torsional cracking shall be assumed to have been provided by the required nominal shear reinforcement. But, where the torsional stiffness (and strength) of members has been considered in the analysis, the members shall be designed for torsion.
d) The provisions of this shall be applicable only when:
1) Solid rectangular beams with D >b,
2) Hollow rectangular beams with D > b and wall thickness t>b/4,
3) Solid T-beams and I-beams, whose webs comply with the requirement of D >b ,and
4) PSC concrete beams have an average intensity of prestress less than 0.3 f,;.
222
context_text.next_page:
FOR BIS USE ONLY
CED 02(27096)P January 2025
e) In concrete structures, where torsional moment is required to maintain equilibrium, members shall be designed for torsion in accordance with the provisions given in 9.3.2.6. But, for such indeterminate concrete structures, where torsional moment can be eliminated by releasing redundant restraints, without compromising the safety of the member and thereby of the structure, no specific design for torsional moment is necessary, provided torsional stiffness is neglected in structural analysis when calculating the internal stress-resultants.
9.3.2.6.2 Limiting stress
A concrete member shall resist the design torsional moment demand Ty at the critical section by concrete, transverse reinforcement steel and longitudinal steel. Under the action of shear force, at the critical section of a reinforced concrete or prestressed concrete member:
a) Contribution of concrete
Concrete shall be assumed to have reached its limiting stress of r, , the design shear strength of concrete; and
b) Contribution of transverse reinforcing steel
Transverse reinforcing steel shall be assumed to have reached its limiting stress of 0.87 f,, the design tensile strength of reinforcing steel.
And, the shear force resisted by the concrete member when the member reaches the said limiting stresses simultaneously at a section shall be taken as the design shear force capacity V,, of that section.
Shear reinforcement shall be provided only in the form of vertical stirrups. Also, adequate control of torsional cracking shall be provided by the shear reinforcement as per 9.3.2.5.2.1.
9.3.2.6.3 Critical section
Sections located less than a distance d from the face of the support may be designed for the same torsional moment as computed at a distance d, where d is the effective depth of the member.
